text.title: Indie Mini-CRM Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that keep a Notion-based indie CRM healthy through 6+ months of use. Hard rules prevent the most common failure: turning the CRM into a graveyard of half-tagged contacts.

rule.statement: Use exactly four linked Notion databases: Contacts, Outreach (each outreach attempt is a row), Partners (affiliates / promo-swaps), Touches (each interaction is a row). Reject schemas with fewer (collapses; can't track outreach attempts) or more (decays in 30 days).
rule.rationale: Three databases miss the distinction between "contact" and "outreach attempt." Five or more drift into unused complexity within a month.

rule.statement: contact_type is a closed select-list: subscriber, customer, partner, prospect, VIP. Reject multi-select for contact_type (one human, one type at a time). VIP is reserved for ≤ 5% of contacts who get personal touch monthly.
rule.rationale: Multi-select tags balloon to 20+ overlapping types in 90 days. Single-select forces a clean rollup and visible filters.

rule.statement: Every contact has a rollup field last_touch_date computed from related Touches. Contacts past type-specific stall thresholds (subscriber 90d, customer 60d, partner 30d, VIP 14d) surface as "stalled" on the today dashboard.
rule.rationale: Stalling is type-specific. VIPs need 14d cadence; subscribers tolerate 90d. Without rollup, stalls hide for months.

rule.statement: Each Outreach row carries: template_id, sent_date, response_status (no_reply / replied / not_interested / converted). After every 20 outreach attempts, compute and display per-template response rate. Reject continued use of a template with response rate < 5% over n ≥ 30 attempts.
rule.rationale: Templates degrade as audiences saturate. Forced 5% floor prevents wasting reach on a stale script.

rule.statement: Once per month, run a one-screen pulse: new subscribers, conversion rate, partner activity, VIP touches, stalled contacts count. If any of the five metrics regress > 25% MoM, schedule a 30-min review the same week.
rule.rationale: Indie operators miss compound declines because each metric individually looks fine. Combined pulse catches the slow-motion crash.

example.title: Four-database schema
example.code:
Contacts(name, contact_type, source, joined_date, last_touch_date_rollup) Outreach(target_contact, template_id, sent_date, response_status, channel) Partners(name, deal_type, status, last_renewal_date, MRR_attributed) Touches(contact, kind, date, note)
Linked: Contacts ↔ Touches (last_touch_date_rollup) Contacts ↔ Outreach (history) Partners ↔ Contacts (affiliate to subscriber)
example.note: Illustrates r1 + r3: four databases, last_touch tracked via rollup.

example.title: Single-database mega-table
example.code:
Everything(name, tags, multi_select_type, status, note, last_action, outreach_template, response, deal_status, MRR, ...)
Result: 22 fields, half empty, tags balloon to 31 unique values in 60 days. Operator abandons in week 11.
example.note: Illustrates r1 violation. Indie CRMs die from "everything in one big table" architecture.
